titleEnglish: Dhyana Yoga - The Yoga of Meditation
description: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6, Dhyana Yoga, is the definitive guide to meditation in Hindu scripture. Krishna begins by declaring that a true sannyasi and yogi is one who performs duty without attachment to results, not one who merely renounces fire rituals or action. He teaches the famous maxim 'Uddhared Atmanatmanam' — one must elevate oneself by one's own self, for the self alone is one's friend or enemy. Detailed instructions on meditation posture, place, diet, and mental focus are given. The mind is compared to a steady lamp in a windless place. When Arjuna confesses that the mind is as difficult to control as the wind, Krishna reassures him that through practice (abhyasa) and detachment (vairagya), the mind can be mastered. The chapter concludes with Krishna's assurance that no spiritual effort is ever wasted, and declares the devoted yogi as the highest of all seekers.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः।
स सन्न्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
Anashritah Karma-Phalam Karyam Karma Karoti Yah.
Sa Sannyasi Cha Yogi Cha Na Nir-Agnir-Na Cha-Akriyah.
verses.hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान बोले — जो कर्मफल का आश्रय न लेकर कर्तव्य कर्म करता है, वही संन्यासी और योगी है; न वह जो अग्नि (यज्ञ) त्यागता है, न वह जो कर्म त्यागता है।
verses.englishTranslation: The Supreme Lord said: One who performs their obligatory duty without depending on the fruits of action is a true sannyasi and yogi — not one who merely renounces fire rituals, nor one who merely renounces action.
verses.commentary: Krishna opens this chapter by redefining both sannyasa and yoga. The true renunciate is not one who abandons rituals or sits idle, but one who performs duty without attachment to results. This revolutionary definition unites the paths of renunciation and action, showing that inner detachment while performing one's duty is the highest spiritual practice.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यं सन्न्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव।
न ह्यसन्न्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन॥
verses.transliteration:
Yam Sannyasam-Iti Prahur-Yogam Tam Viddhi Pandava.
Na Hy-Asannyasta-Sankalpo Yogi Bhavati Kashchana.
verses.hindiTranslation: हे पाण्डव! जिसे संन्यास कहते हैं, उसे ही योग जानो। बिना संकल्पों (स्वार्थी इच्छाओं) का त्याग किए कोई भी योगी नहीं बन सकता।
verses.englishTranslation: Know that what they call renunciation (sannyasa) is the same as yoga, O Pandava. No one becomes a yogi without renouncing selfish resolve (sankalpa).
verses.commentary: Krishna establishes the essential identity between sannyasa and yoga — both require the renunciation of selfish desire (sankalpa). Without giving up personal agendas and self-centered motivations, no one can truly practice yoga. This verse underscores that the internal attitude of desirelessness is the common foundation of all spiritual paths.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Arurukshor-Muner-Yogam Karma Karanam-Uchyate.
Yoga-Arudhasya Tasya-Iva Shamah Karanam-Uchyate.

wordByWord.meaning: for one who has attained yoga

verses.hindiTranslation: योग में आरोहण (चढ़ने) की इच्छा रखने वाले मुनि के लिए कर्म कारण (साधन) कहा जाता है; योगारूढ (योग में स्थित) के लिए शम (शान्ति/कर्म-उपरति) कारण कहा जाता है।
verses.englishTranslation: For the sage aspiring to attain yoga, action is said to be the means. For one who has attained yoga, tranquility (cessation of action) is said to be the means.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes two stages of the spiritual journey. For the beginner seeking to attain yoga, selfless action is the primary tool for purification and preparation. For the advanced practitioner who has already established themselves in yoga, inner tranquility and cessation of restless activity becomes the means for deeper realization. This teaches that the spiritual path naturally evolves from active engagement to meditative stillness.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसन्न्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Yada Hi Na-Indriya-Artheshu Na Karmasv-Anushajjate.
Sarva-Sankalpa-Sannyasi Yoga-Arudhas-Tado-Uchyate.
verses.hindiTranslation: जब मनुष्य न इन्द्रियों के विषयों में और न कर्मों में आसक्त होता है, सब संकल्पों का त्याग कर देता है — तब वह योगारूढ कहलाता है।
verses.englishTranslation: When one is not attached to sense objects or to actions, having renounced all selfish resolve — then that person is said to be established in yoga.
verses.commentary: Krishna defines the state of being yoga-arudha (established in yoga). It is characterized by complete non-attachment to both sensory pleasures and actions, combined with the renunciation of all selfish intentions. This state is not about physical inactivity but about the complete absence of ego-driven motivation. It is the mark of spiritual maturity.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत्।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
Uddhared-Atmana-Atmanam Na-Atmanam-Avasadayet.
Atma-Iva Hy-Atmano Bandhur-Atma-Iva Ripur-Atmanah.
verses.hindiTranslation: मनुष्य को अपने आप को स्वयं ऊपर उठाना चाहिए, अपने आप को गिरने नहीं देना चाहिए। क्योंकि आत्मा ही आत्मा का बन्धु है और आत्मा ही आत्मा का शत्रु है।
verses.englishTranslation: One should uplift oneself by one's own self and not degrade oneself. For the self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
verses.commentary: This is one of the most empowering verses in the Gita. Krishna places the responsibility and the power of spiritual evolution squarely on the individual. No external savior is needed — the self has the capacity to be its own greatest friend or its own worst enemy. This teaching emphasizes personal responsibility, self-effort, and the innate potential within every being for self-transformation.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत्॥
verses.transliteration:
Bandhur-Atma-Atmanas-Tasya Yena-Atma-Iva-Atmana Jitah.
Anatmanas-Tu Shatrutve Varteta-Atma-Iva Shatruvat.

wordByWord.meaning: of one who has not conquered the self

verses.hindiTranslation: जिसने अपने आत्मा (मन) को आत्मा (विवेक) द्वारा जीत लिया है, उसके लिए आत्मा बन्धु है। पर जिसने अपने आप को नहीं जीता, उसके लिए आत्मा शत्रु की भाँति व्यवहार करता है।
verses.englishTranslation: For one who has conquered the self by the self, the self is a friend. But for one who has not conquered the self, the self acts as an enemy.
verses.commentary: Expanding on the previous verse, Krishna explains when the self becomes a friend and when an enemy. The conquered (disciplined) self is one's greatest ally, supporting spiritual growth and inner peace. The unconquered (undisciplined) self becomes one's worst adversary, leading to self-destructive behavior. This simple yet profound teaching reveals that our relationship with our own mind determines our destiny.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः॥
verses.transliteration:
Jita-Atmanah Prashantasya Parama-Atma Samahitah.
Shito-Ushna-Sukha-Duhkheshu Tatha Mana-Apamanayoh.

wordByWord.meaning: of one who has conquered the self

verses.hindiTranslation: जिसने अपने आप को जीत लिया है और जो प्रशान्त है, उसका परमात्मा (अन्तरात्मा) सुस्थित है — सर्दी-गर्मी, सुख-दुःख तथा मान-अपमान में।
verses.englishTranslation: For one who has conquered the self and is tranquil, the Supreme Self is steady — in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, honor and dishonor.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes the state of the self-conquered person: their awareness of the Supreme Self remains undisturbed by the three pairs of opposites — physical (cold-heat), emotional (pleasure-pain), and social (honor-dishonor). This equanimity is not indifference but a profound stability rooted in the realization that the true Self is beyond all changing conditions.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः॥
verses.transliteration:
Jnana-Vijnana-Tripta-Atma Kutastho Vijite-Indriyah.
Yukta Ity-Uchyate Yogi Sama-Loshta-Ashma-Kanchanah.

wordByWord.meaning: equal towards a clod, stone, and gold

verses.hindiTranslation: ज्ञान और विज्ञान (अनुभवजन्य ज्ञान) से तृप्त, अविचल, जितेन्द्रिय — ऐसा योगी 'युक्त' कहलाता है; जो मिट्टी के ढेले, पत्थर और सोने को समान देखता है।
verses.englishTranslation: Satisfied by knowledge and realized wisdom, unchanging, with senses conquered — such a yogi is said to be united. One who looks equally upon a clod of earth, a stone, and gold.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes the fully realized yogi who is satisfied within by both theoretical knowledge (jnana) and direct experience (vijnana). Such a person is kutastha — immovable like an anvil that remains unchanged despite being struck. The remarkable equanimity of seeing equal value in a clod of earth, a stone, and gold demonstrates complete freedom from material attachment.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
सुहृन्मित्रार्युदासीनमध्यस्थद्वेष्यबन्धुषु।
साधुष्वपि च पापेषु समबुद्धिर्विशिष्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Suhrin-Mitra-Ary-Udasina-Madhyastha-Dveshya-Bandhushu.
Sadhushv-Api Cha Papeshu Sama-Buddhir-Vishishyate.
verses.hindiTranslation: सुहृद, मित्र, शत्रु, उदासीन, मध्यस्थ, द्वेषी और बन्धुओं में, तथा साधु और पापियों में — समान बुद्धि रखने वाला विशिष्ट (श्रेष्ठ) है।
verses.englishTranslation: One who maintains equal vision towards well-wishers, friends, enemies, the indifferent, the neutral, the hateful, relatives, the righteous, and the sinful — that person excels.
verses.commentary: Krishna lists seven categories of human relationships and declares that maintaining equal vision across all of them is the mark of spiritual excellence. This does not mean treating everyone identically in behavior, but seeing the same divine Self in all beings regardless of their relationship to oneself. This sama-buddhi (equal vision) is the practical expression of the highest spiritual realization.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः॥
verses.transliteration:
Yogi Yunjita Satatam-Atmanam Rahasi Sthitah.
Ekaki Yata-Chitta-Atma Nirashir-Aparigrahah.
verses.hindiTranslation: योगी एकान्त में स्थित होकर, अकेला, मन और शरीर को वश में करके, आशारहित और परिग्रहरहित होकर सतत अपने आत्मा (मन) को एकाग्र करे।
verses.englishTranslation: The yogi should constantly concentrate the mind, staying in a secluded place, alone, with mind and body controlled, free from desires and possessiveness.
verses.commentary: Krishna now begins the practical instructions for meditation. Five conditions are prescribed: solitude (rahasi), being alone (ekaki), self-control (yata-chitta-atma), desirelessness (nirashih), and non-possessiveness (aparigrahah). These external and internal conditions create the ideal environment for the mind to turn inward and rest in the Self.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Shuchau Deshe Pratishthapya Sthiram-Asanam-Atmanah.
Na-Aty-Uchchhritam Na-Ati-Nicham Chaila-Ajina-Kusho-Uttaram.

wordByWord.meaning: cloth, deerskin, and kusha grass layered

verses.hindiTranslation: शुद्ध (पवित्र) स्थान पर अपना स्थिर आसन जमाकर — जो न बहुत ऊँचा हो न बहुत नीचा, जिस पर कुशा, मृगचर्म और वस्त्र बिछा हो —
verses.englishTranslation: In a clean place, having established a firm seat for oneself — neither too high nor too low — covered with kusha grass, a deerskin, and a cloth —
verses.commentary: Krishna gives specific instructions for the meditation seat. The place should be pure and clean. The seat should be stable, neither too elevated (which causes instability) nor too low (which allows insects and dampness). The traditional layering of kusha grass, deerskin, and cloth creates an insulated, comfortable seat. These practical details show Krishna's concern for making meditation accessible.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये॥
verses.transliteration:
Tatra-Ikagram Manah Kritva Yata-Chitta-Indriya-Kriyah.
Upavishya-Asane Yunjyad-Yogam-Atma-Vishuddhaye.

wordByWord.meaning: with controlled mind, senses, and activities

verses.hindiTranslation: वहाँ आसन पर बैठकर, मन को एकाग्र करके, चित्त और इन्द्रियों की क्रियाओं को संयमित करके, आत्मशुद्धि के लिए योग का अभ्यास करे।
verses.englishTranslation: Seated there on the seat, making the mind one-pointed, with the activities of the mind and senses controlled, one should practice yoga for self-purification.
verses.commentary: The purpose of meditation is clearly stated: atma-vishuddhaye — for the purification of the self. The practice involves sitting on the prepared seat, making the mind one-pointed (ekagra), and controlling the activities of the mind and senses. This systematic approach — proper seat, focused mind, controlled senses — forms the foundation of all meditation practices in yoga.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्॥
verses.transliteration:
Samam Kaya-Shiro-Grivam Dharayann-Achalam Sthirah.
Samprekshya Nasika-Agram Svam Dishah-Cha-An-Avalokayan.
verses.hindiTranslation: शरीर, सिर और गर्दन को सीधा, स्थिर और अचल रखकर, अपनी नासिका के अग्रभाग पर दृष्टि रखते हुए, इधर-उधर न देखते हुए —
verses.englishTranslation: Holding the body, head, and neck erect, still and firm, gazing at the tip of one's own nose, without looking around —
verses.commentary: Krishna provides specific physical instructions for meditation posture: the spine, head, and neck should be aligned and held still. The gaze is directed toward the tip of the nose (nasikagra), which helps concentrate the mind. Not looking around prevents distraction. This erect, still posture facilitates the flow of prana and creates the physical foundation for deep meditation.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः॥
verses.transliteration:
Prashanta-Atma Vigata-Bhir-Brahmachari-Vrate Sthitah.
Manah Samyamya Mach-Chitto Yukta Asita Mat-Parah.

wordByWord.meaning: having Me as the supreme goal

verses.hindiTranslation: प्रशान्त आत्मा वाला, भयरहित, ब्रह्मचर्य व्रत में स्थित, मन को संयमित करके, मुझमें चित्त लगाकर, मुझे ही परम लक्ष्य मानकर योगयुक्त होकर बैठे।
verses.englishTranslation: With mind at peace, free from fear, established in the vow of celibacy, controlling the mind, with thoughts fixed on Me — one should sit engaged in yoga, devoted to Me as the supreme goal.
verses.commentary: Krishna adds the inner conditions to the outer posture: peaceful mind, fearlessness, celibacy (brahmacharya), mental control, and most importantly, devotion to God as the ultimate focus. The instruction 'mat-chittah' and 'mat-parah' (mind on Me, devoted to Me) introduces a personal, devotional element into the practice of meditation. Yoga is not just technique but a loving connection with the Divine.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
Yunjann-Evam Sada-Atmanam Yogi Niyata-Manasah.
Shantim Nirvana-Paramam Mat-Samstham-Adhigachchhati.
verses.hindiTranslation: इस प्रकार सदा अपने मन को एकाग्र करता हुआ, नियतमानस (अनुशासित मन वाला) योगी मुझमें स्थित निर्वाण-परम शान्ति को प्राप्त होता है।
verses.englishTranslation: Thus constantly concentrating the mind, the yogi with a disciplined mind attains the supreme peace of nirvana, abiding in Me.
verses.commentary: Krishna reveals the fruit of consistent meditation practice: the supreme peace that is nirvana itself, and which abides in God (mat-samstham). This peace is not temporary relief but the ultimate liberation. The emphasis on 'sada' (always) and 'niyata-manasah' (disciplined mind) underscores that meditation requires regular, sustained practice for its full benefit to unfold.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः।
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
Na-Aty-Ashnatas-Tu Yogo'sti Na Chaika-Antam-Anashnatah.
Na Cha-Ati-Svapna-Shilasya Jagrato Naiva Cha-Arjuna.

wordByWord.meaning: of one who eats too much
of one who does not eat
of one who sleeps too much
of one who stays awake too much

verses.hindiTranslation: हे अर्जुन! न अधिक खाने वाले का योग सिद्ध होता है, न बिलकुल न खाने वाले का; न अधिक सोने वाले का और न सदा जागने वाले का।
verses.englishTranslation: Yoga is not for one who eats too much, nor for one who does not eat at all, O Arjuna. It is not for one who sleeps too much, nor for one who stays awake too much.
verses.commentary: Krishna teaches the principle of moderation that is essential for successful yoga practice. Extremes of any kind — whether overeating or fasting, oversleeping or sleep deprivation — are obstacles to meditation. This practical wisdom shows that yoga is not about extreme asceticism but about finding the balanced middle path that supports both physical health and spiritual practice.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा॥
verses.transliteration:
Yukta-Ahara-Viharasya Yukta-Cheshtasya Karmasu.
Yukta-Svapna-Avabodhasya Yogo Bhavati Duhkha-Ha.

wordByWord.meaning: of one whose eating and recreation are balanced
of one whose efforts in action are balanced
of one whose sleep and waking are balanced

verses.hindiTranslation: जो आहार-विहार में संतुलित है, कर्मों में संतुलित प्रयास करता है, सोने और जागने में संतुलित है — उसका योग दुःखनाशक होता है।
verses.englishTranslation: For one who is balanced in eating and recreation, balanced in effort in actions, and balanced in sleep and waking — yoga becomes the destroyer of suffering.
verses.commentary: This celebrated verse prescribes the balanced lifestyle essential for yoga. The threefold balance covers diet and leisure (yukta-ahara-vihara), work effort (yukta-cheshta), and rest patterns (yukta-svapna-avabodha). When life is lived in this harmonious balance, yoga naturally destroys all suffering. This teaching has universal relevance, showing that spiritual life flourishes through moderation, not extremes.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा॥
verses.transliteration:
Yada Viniyatam Chittam-Atmany-Eva-Avatishthate.
Nih-Sprihah Sarva-Kamebhyo Yukta Ity-Uchyate Tada.
verses.hindiTranslation: जब पूर्णतया नियमित चित्त आत्मा में ही स्थित हो जाता है, और सब कामनाओं से निःस्पृह हो जाता है — तब वह 'युक्त' (योग में स्थित) कहलाता है।
verses.englishTranslation: When the fully disciplined mind rests in the Self alone, free from longing for all desires — then one is said to be united (yukta) in yoga.
verses.commentary: Krishna defines the state of yoga precisely: the mind rests exclusively in the Self, without being pulled by any desire. This is not suppression of desires but a natural state where the mind has found such complete fulfillment in the Self that external desires simply lose their appeal. This verse describes the goal of all meditation practice.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
Yatha Dipo Nivata-Stho Ne-Ingate So-Upama Smrita.
Yogino Yata-Chittasya Yunjato Yogam-Atmanah.
verses.hindiTranslation: जैसे वायुरहित स्थान में दीपक की लौ नहीं हिलती — ऐसी ही उपमा दी गई है उस योगी की जिसका चित्त वश में है और जो आत्मा का योग कर रहा है।
verses.englishTranslation: As a lamp in a windless place does not flicker — such is the simile given for the yogi of controlled mind, practicing yoga of the Self.
verses.commentary: This is one of the most beautiful and memorable similes in the Gita. The steady flame of a lamp in a place without wind perfectly illustrates the still, unwavering mind of a yogi in deep meditation. Just as the undisturbed flame gives perfect, clear light, the concentrated mind of a meditator reveals the Self in its full clarity. This image has inspired meditators for millennia.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
Yatra-Uparamate Chittam Niruddham Yoga-Sevaya.
Yatra Chaiva-Atmana-Atmanam Pashyann-Atmani Tushyati.
verses.hindiTranslation: जहाँ योग के अभ्यास से निरुद्ध (रोका गया) चित्त उपराम (शान्त) हो जाता है, और जहाँ स्वयं आत्मा द्वारा आत्मा को देखकर आत्मा में सन्तुष्ट होता है —
verses.englishTranslation: Where the mind, restrained by the practice of yoga, becomes quiet; where, seeing the Self by the self, one is satisfied in the Self —
verses.commentary: Krishna begins describing the state of samadhi (verses 20-23). The mind becomes completely still through yoga practice and ceases all its usual fluctuations. In this stillness, the purified mind becomes the mirror in which the Self sees itself. The resulting satisfaction is complete and self-sufficient — it needs nothing external. This is the beginning of the highest meditative experience.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम्।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः॥
verses.transliteration:
Sukham-Atyantikam Yat-Tad Buddhi-Grahyam-Atindriyam.
Vetti Yatra Na Chaiva-Ayam Sthitash-Chalati Tattvatah.
verses.hindiTranslation: जिसमें अत्यन्तिक (असीम) सुख जो बुद्धि से ग्रहण होने योग्य और इन्द्रियों से परे है, उसे अनुभव करता है — और जहाँ स्थित होकर तत्त्व से विचलित नहीं होता —
verses.englishTranslation: In which one experiences infinite happiness that is grasped by the intellect and beyond the senses — and where, once established, one never deviates from the truth —
verses.commentary: Continuing the description of samadhi, Krishna describes the infinite bliss (atyantikam sukham) that is experienced in deep meditation. This happiness is suprasensory (atindriyam) — it cannot be perceived by the physical senses but only by the refined intellect. Once established in this experience, the yogi never deviates from truth. This is a happiness infinitely greater than any sensory pleasure.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Yam Labdhva Cha-Aparam Labham Manyate Na-Adhikam Tatah.
Yasmin-Sthito Na Duhkhena Guruna-Api Vichalyate.
verses.hindiTranslation: जिसे प्राप्त करके उससे अधिक कोई अन्य लाभ नहीं मानता; और जिसमें स्थित होकर भारी दुःख से भी विचलित नहीं होता —
verses.englishTranslation: Having gained which, one considers no other gain to be greater; and established in which, one is not shaken even by the heaviest sorrow —
verses.commentary: The state of yoga is described as the supreme gain — nothing in all of existence is considered more valuable. Moreover, once established in this state, even the heaviest grief cannot shake the yogi. This unshakable stability in the face of life's greatest challenges demonstrates the transformative power of yoga. It is not mere philosophical understanding but an experiential state of invulnerability.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम्।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा॥
verses.transliteration:
Tam Vidyad-Duhkha-Samyoga-Viyogam Yoga-Samjnitam.
Sa Nishchayena Yoktavyo Yogo'nirvin-Na-Chetasa.
verses.hindiTranslation: जो दुःख के संयोग (सम्पर्क) से विच्छेद है, उसे योग के नाम से जानो। वह योग निश्चय (दृढ़ संकल्प) से और बिना उकताए (अनिर्विण्ण) चित्त से करना चाहिए।
verses.englishTranslation: Know that state of disconnection from the union with sorrow as what is called yoga. That yoga should be practiced with determination and an undaunted mind.
verses.commentary: Krishna provides a beautiful definition of yoga: it is the state of disconnection from sorrow (duhkha-samyoga-viyoga). Then He prescribes two essential attitudes for practice: firm determination (nishchaya) and an undaunted spirit (anirvinna-chetasa) that does not become discouraged by difficulties. Yoga requires both resolve and patience — one must persist cheerfully through all obstacles.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
सङ्कल्पप्रभवान्कामांस्त्यक्त्वा सर्वानशेषतः।
मनसैवेन्द्रियग्रामं विनियम्य समन्ततः॥
verses.transliteration:
Sankalpa-Prabhavan Kamams-Tyaktva Sarvan-Asheshatah.
Manasa-Eva-Indriya-Gramam Viniyamya Samantatah.
verses.hindiTranslation: संकल्प (मन की कल्पना) से उत्पन्न सभी कामनाओं को पूर्णतया त्यागकर, मन द्वारा ही इन्द्रियों के समूह को सब ओर से संयमित करके —
verses.englishTranslation: Completely abandoning all desires born of mental resolve, and fully restraining the group of senses from all sides by the mind alone —
verses.commentary: Krishna identifies the root of desire: sankalpa, or mental imagination and resolve. All desires begin as thoughts in the mind, and they must be abandoned at that source. The senses are then restrained not by force but by the disciplined mind itself (manasa eva). This verse teaches the correct order of practice: first dissolve mental desires, then naturally the senses come under control.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
शनैः शनैरुपरमेद्बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया।
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
Shanaih Shanair-Uparamed-Buddhya Dhriti-Grihitaya.
Atma-Samstham Manah Kritva Na Kinchid-Api Chintayet.
verses.hindiTranslation: धीरे-धीरे, धृति (धैर्य) से युक्त बुद्धि द्वारा उपराम (शान्त) हो। मन को आत्मा में स्थापित करके किसी भी वस्तु का चिन्तन न करे।
verses.englishTranslation: Gradually, step by step, one should become still with the intellect held firm by conviction. Establishing the mind in the Self, one should not think of anything at all.
verses.commentary: This practical instruction for meditation emphasizes gradualness (shanaih shanaih). The mind cannot be forced into stillness — it must be gently led with the support of a firm, patient intellect. The ultimate state is one where the mind rests in the Self and all thought-waves cease. This instruction 'na kinchid api chintayet' (think of nothing at all) describes the highest state of meditation where even the subtlest thoughts dissolve.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यतो यतो निश्चरति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम्।
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
Yato Yato Nishcharati Manash-Chanchalam-Asthiram.
Tatas-Tato Niyamya-Etad-Atmany-Eva Vasham Nayet.
verses.hindiTranslation: चञ्चल और अस्थिर मन जहाँ-जहाँ भटकता है, वहाँ-वहाँ से उसे रोककर आत्मा में ही वश में लाए।
verses.englishTranslation: Wherever the restless and unsteady mind wanders, from there one should restrain it and bring it back under the control of the Self alone.
verses.commentary: This is one of the most practical meditation instructions in all of scripture. Krishna acknowledges that the mind is naturally restless and unsteady — this is not a failure but a reality to work with. The practice is simple yet powerful: each time the mind wanders, gently bring it back to the Self. This patient, persistent redirection is the actual practice of meditation. There is no shortcut.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम्।
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Prashanta-Manasam Hy-Enam Yoginam Sukham-Uttamam.
Upaiti Shanta-Rajasam Brahma-Bhutam-Akalmasham.
verses.hindiTranslation: जिसका मन प्रशान्त है, रजोगुण शान्त हो गया है, जो निष्पाप (अकल्मष) और ब्रह्मभूत हो गया है — उस योगी को उत्तम सुख स्वयं आ मिलता है।
verses.englishTranslation: Supreme happiness comes to the yogi whose mind is peaceful, whose passion is calmed, who has become one with Brahman, and who is free from sin.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes the natural result of successful meditation: supreme happiness spontaneously approaches the yogi. Note the passive construction — happiness 'comes to' the yogi rather than being pursued. When the mind is at peace, passion is stilled, one has become Brahman, and all impurities are removed, the highest bliss is the natural consequence. This is the reward of persistent meditation practice.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी विगतकल्मषः।
सुखेन ब्रह्मसंस्पर्शमत्यन्तं सुखमश्नुते॥
verses.transliteration:
Yunjann-Evam Sada-Atmanam Yogi Vigata-Kalmashah.
Sukhena Brahma-Samsparsham-Atyantam Sukham-Ashnute.
verses.hindiTranslation: इस प्रकार सदा आत्मा को योग में लगाता हुआ, पापरहित योगी सहज ही ब्रह्म-संस्पर्श (ब्रह्म से सम्पर्क) का अत्यन्त सुख अनुभव करता है।
verses.englishTranslation: Thus constantly engaging the self in yoga, the yogi freed from impurities easily enjoys the infinite happiness of contact with Brahman.
verses.commentary: The purified yogi who constantly practices meditation experiences the supreme bliss of Brahman-contact (brahma-samsparsha) with ease (sukhena). The word 'sukhena' is significant — for the purified practitioner, the experience of divine bliss comes naturally and effortlessly. This is the fruit of sustained practice and inner purification.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थमात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि।
ईक्षते योगयुक्तात्मा सर्वत्र समदर्शनः॥
verses.transliteration:
Sarva-Bhuta-Stham-Atmanam Sarva-Bhutani Cha-Atmani.
Ikshate Yoga-Yukta-Atma Sarvatra Sama-Darshanah.
verses.hindiTranslation: योगयुक्त आत्मा वाला सब प्राणियों में आत्मा को और आत्मा में सब प्राणियों को देखता है — वह सर्वत्र समदर्शी होता है।
verses.englishTranslation: One whose self is united in yoga sees the Self dwelling in all beings and all beings in the Self — seeing equally everywhere.
verses.commentary: This verse describes the vision of the perfected yogi: seeing the one Self in all beings and all beings in the one Self. This is not a philosophical concept but a direct experiential vision that arises from deep meditation. The yogi sees the same divine essence in every creature, object, and situation. This sama-darshana (equal vision) is the natural fruit of yoga and the foundation of universal compassion.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति।
तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
Yo Mam Pashyati Sarvatra Sarvam Cha Mayi Pashyati.
Tasya-Aham Na Pranashyami Sa Cha Me Na Pranashyati.
verses.hindiTranslation: जो मुझे सर्वत्र देखता है और सब कुछ मुझमें देखता है — उसके लिए मैं कभी अदृश्य नहीं होता और वह मेरे लिए कभी अदृश्य नहीं होता।
verses.englishTranslation: One who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me — I am never lost to such a person, nor is that person ever lost to Me.
verses.commentary: This deeply personal and devotional verse reveals the intimate relationship between God and the devotee who has realized universal vision. When one sees Krishna everywhere and everything in Krishna, an unbreakable bond is established. God promises never to be absent from such a devotee's awareness, and the devotee never falls from God's grace. This mutual 'never-losing' is the highest expression of divine love.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि स योगी मयि वर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
Sarva-Bhuta-Sthitam Yo Mam Bhajaty-Ekatvam-Asthitah.
Sarvatha Vartamano'pi Sa Yogi Mayi Vartate.
verses.hindiTranslation: जो एकत्व में स्थित होकर सब प्राणियों में स्थित मुझे भजता है — वह योगी सब प्रकार से व्यवहार करता हुआ भी मुझमें ही वर्तमान (स्थित) है।
verses.englishTranslation: One who, established in unity, worships Me as dwelling in all beings — that yogi, however they may live and act, abides in Me.
verses.commentary: Krishna reveals a remarkable truth: the yogi who worships God as present in all beings, established in the consciousness of oneness, always abides in God regardless of external circumstances or activities. This means spiritual realization does not require withdrawal from the world — one can engage in any activity and still remain in God-consciousness. This is the ultimate freedom of the realized soul.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
आत्मौपम्येन सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन।
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः॥
verses.transliteration:
Atma-Aupamyena Sarvatra Samam Pashyati Yo'rjuna.
Sukham Va Yadi Va Duhkham Sa Yogi Paramo Matah.
verses.hindiTranslation: हे अर्जुन! जो अपनी तुलना से (आत्मौपम्य) सर्वत्र समान रूप से देखता है — चाहे सुख हो या दुःख — वह योगी परम (सर्वश्रेष्ठ) माना गया है।
verses.englishTranslation: O Arjuna, one who sees equally everywhere — whether happiness or sorrow — by comparing others with oneself, is considered the supreme yogi.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes the supreme yogi as one who practices 'atmaupamya' — seeing others through the lens of one's own experience. Just as I desire happiness and fear pain, so do all beings. This empathetic identification with all creatures, feeling their joy and sorrow as one's own, is the highest yoga. It is essentially the golden rule elevated to a cosmic vision of unity.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन।
एतस्याहं न पश्यामि चञ्चलत्वात्स्थितिं स्थिराम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Yo'yam Yogas-Tvaya Proktah Samyena Madhusudana.
Etasya-Aham Na Pashyami Chanchalatavat Sthitim Sthiram.
verses.hindiTranslation: अर्जुन बोले — हे मधुसूदन! जो यह समत्व-आधारित योग आपने बताया है — मन की चंचलता के कारण मैं इसकी स्थिर स्थिति नहीं देख पाता।
verses.englishTranslation: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana, this yoga of equanimity that You have described — I do not see how it can have a firm foundation, due to the restlessness of the mind.
verses.commentary: Arjuna raises the most human and relatable of all spiritual questions: how can the mind, which is inherently restless, ever achieve the perfect equanimity Krishna has described? This honest confession resonates with every spiritual seeker who has struggled with mental discipline. Arjuna's sincerity in admitting his difficulty makes him an ideal student and invites Krishna's compassionate guidance.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम्।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Chanchalam Hi Manah Krishna Pramathi Balavad Dridham.
Tasya-Aham Nigraham Manye Vayor-Iva Su-Dushkaram.
verses.hindiTranslation: हे कृष्ण! मन तो चञ्चल, प्रमथनकारी (उद्वेलित करने वाला), बलवान और दृढ़ है। मैं इसके निग्रह (नियन्त्रण) को वायु के निग्रह के समान अत्यन्त कठिन मानता हूँ।
verses.englishTranslation: The mind is indeed restless, O Krishna — turbulent, strong, and obstinate. I consider controlling it as difficult as controlling the wind.
verses.commentary: Arjuna gives a vivid and universally relatable description of the mind's nature: chanchala (restless), pramathi (turbulent, violently agitating), balavat (powerful), and dridha (obstinate). His comparison of mind-control to wind-control perfectly captures the seeming impossibility of the task. This verse validates every meditator's experience and sets up Krishna's reassuring response about the practical path to mastery.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम्।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Asamshayam Maha-Baho Mano Dur-Nigraham Chalam.
Abhyasena Tu Kaunteya Vairagyena Cha Grihyate.
verses.hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान बोले — हे महाबाहो! निःसंदेह मन चञ्चल और दुर्निग्रह (कठिनाई से वश में होने वाला) है। परन्तु हे कौन्तेय! अभ्यास और वैराग्य से यह वश में किया जा सकता है।
verses.englishTranslation: The Supreme Lord said: Undoubtedly, O mighty-armed, the mind is restless and difficult to control. But, O Kaunteya, it can be mastered through practice (abhyasa) and detachment (vairagya).
verses.commentary: Krishna does not deny Arjuna's observation — He fully agrees that the mind is difficult to control. But then He gives the definitive solution: abhyasa (repeated practice) and vairagya (detachment/dispassion). These twin disciplines are the universal prescription for mastering the mind, echoed by Patanjali's Yoga Sutras. Persistent, patient practice combined with growing detachment from worldly allurements gradually tames even the wildest mind.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः॥
verses.transliteration:
Asamyata-Atmana Yogo Dushprapa Iti Me Matih.
Vashya-Atmana Tu Yatata Shakyo'vaptum-Upayatah.
verses.hindiTranslation: मेरे मत में, असंयत (अनियन्त्रित) आत्मा वाले के लिए योग दुष्प्राप्य (कठिन) है। परन्तु वश में किए हुए आत्मा (मन) वाले प्रयत्नशील व्यक्ति द्वारा उचित उपाय से प्राप्त हो सकता है।
verses.englishTranslation: In My opinion, yoga is difficult to attain for one whose self is uncontrolled. But for one whose self is controlled and who strives, it is possible to attain through proper means.
verses.commentary: Krishna is realistic — yoga is indeed difficult for the undisciplined, but achievable for the disciplined person who makes proper effort using the right methods. The key word is 'upayatah' (by proper means), indicating that correct technique matters alongside effort. This balanced view neither dismisses the difficulty nor creates despair — it encourages intelligent, sustained effort.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
Ayatih Shraddhayo-Peto Yogach-Chalita-Manasah.
Aprapya Yoga-Samsiddhim Kam Gatim Krishna Gachchhati.

wordByWord.meaning: one who fails to strive/is negligent

verses.hindiTranslation: अर्जुन बोले — हे कृष्ण! जो श्रद्धा रखता है पर प्रयत्न में शिथिल हो जाता है, जिसका मन योग से विचलित हो जाता है, और जो योगसिद्धि प्राप्त नहीं कर पाता — वह किस गति को प्राप्त होता है?
verses.englishTranslation: Arjuna said: O Krishna, what is the fate of one who has faith but lacks effort, whose mind deviates from yoga, and who fails to attain perfection in yoga?
verses.commentary: Arjuna raises the fear that haunts every spiritual aspirant: what if I start the path but fail to reach the goal? What becomes of the sincere seeker who has faith but falls short of perfection? This deeply practical question reflects Arjuna's concern that incomplete spiritual effort might leave one with neither worldly success nor spiritual liberation.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
कच्चिन्नोभयविभ्रष्टश्छिन्नाभ्रमिव नश्यति।
अप्रतिष्ठो महाबाहो विमूढो ब्रह्मणः पथि॥
verses.transliteration:
Kachchin-Na-Ubhaya-Vibhrashtash-Chhinna-Abhram-Iva Nashyati.
Apratishto Maha-Baho Vimudho Brahmanah Pathi.
verses.hindiTranslation: हे महाबाहो! क्या ब्रह्म के मार्ग पर भ्रमित, दोनों ओर से भ्रष्ट — वह छिन्न मेघ (टूटे बादल) की तरह निराधार होकर नष्ट नहीं हो जाता?
verses.englishTranslation: O mighty-armed, does such a person, fallen from both paths, bewildered on the path to Brahman, not perish like a torn cloud — without any foundation?
verses.commentary: Arjuna's beautiful and poignant metaphor of a 'torn cloud' perfectly captures the fear of spiritual failure. A cloud torn from its group dissipates without producing rain — it neither remained with its group nor reached the earth. Similarly, Arjuna fears that the failed yogi might lose both worldly pleasures (having renounced them) and spiritual liberation (having failed to achieve it), left with nothing.

verses.speaker: Arjuna
verses.devanagari:
एतन्मे संशयं कृष्ण छेत्तुमर्हस्यशेषतः।
त्वदन्यः संशयस्यास्य छेत्ता न ह्युपपद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
Etan-Me Samshayam Krishna Chhettum-Arhasy-Asheshatah.
Tvad-Anyah Samshayasya-Asya Chhetta Na Hy-Upapadyate.
verses.hindiTranslation: हे कृष्ण! आपको इस मेरे संशय को पूर्णतया छेदन (दूर) करना उचित है। आपके अतिरिक्त इस संशय को दूर करने वाला कोई नहीं मिल सकता।
verses.englishTranslation: O Krishna, You should completely dispel this doubt of mine. No one other than You is capable of removing this doubt.
verses.commentary: Arjuna expresses complete faith in Krishna as the sole authority who can resolve his spiritual doubt. This combination of sincere doubt and total faith in the teacher is the ideal attitude for a spiritual seeker. Arjuna's humility in acknowledging that only Krishna can answer this question demonstrates the proper guru-disciple relationship that allows the deepest teachings to flow.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
Partha Naiveha Na-Amutra Vinashas-Tasya Vidyate.
Na Hi Kalyana-Krit-Kashchid-Durgatim Tata Gachchhati.
verses.hindiTranslation: श्रीभगवान बोले — हे पार्थ! न इस लोक में न परलोक में उसका विनाश होता है। हे तात (प्रिय)! कल्याण करने वाला कोई भी दुर्गति को नहीं प्राप्त होता।
verses.englishTranslation: The Supreme Lord said: O Partha, there is no destruction for such a person either in this world or the next. Indeed, dear one, no one who does good ever comes to a bad end.
verses.commentary: This is one of the most comforting and reassuring verses in all scripture. Krishna tenderly addresses Arjuna as 'tata' (dear one/friend) and gives an absolute assurance: no spiritual effort ever goes to waste. The failed yogi does not perish in either world. The universal principle is declared — no one who acts with good intention ever meets with a bad fate. This removes the greatest fear from the spiritual path.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
प्राप्य पुण्यकृतां लोकानुषित्वा शाश्वतीः समाः।
शुचीनां श्रीमतां गेहे योगभ्रष्टोऽभिजायते॥
verses.transliteration:
Prapya Punya-Kritam Lokan-Ushitva Shashvatih Samah.
Shuchinam Shrimatam Gehe Yoga-Bhrashto'bhijayate.
verses.hindiTranslation: योगभ्रष्ट (योग से गिरा हुआ) व्यक्ति पुण्यवानों के लोकों को प्राप्त करके अनेक वर्ष वहाँ रहकर, पवित्र और श्रीमान (समृद्ध) कुल में जन्म लेता है।
verses.englishTranslation: Having attained the worlds of the meritorious and having dwelt there for many years, one who has fallen from yoga is reborn in the house of the pure and prosperous.
verses.commentary: Krishna describes the fate of the yoga-bhrashta (one who falls from yoga). Far from being destroyed, such a person first enjoys the heavenly realms earned by their spiritual merit, and then takes birth in a pure, wealthy family. This favorable rebirth provides the ideal circumstances for continuing their spiritual journey. No spiritual effort is ever lost — it carries forward across lifetimes.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
अथवा योगिनामेव कुले भवति धीमताम्।
एतद्धि दुर्लभतरं लोके जन्म यदीदृशम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Athava Yoginam-Eva Kule Bhavati Dhimatam.
Etad-Dhi Durlabha-Taram Loke Janma Yad-Idrisham.
verses.hindiTranslation: अथवा बुद्धिमान योगियों के कुल में ही जन्म लेता है। संसार में ऐसा जन्म अत्यन्त दुर्लभ है।
verses.englishTranslation: Or one is born into a family of wise yogis. Indeed, such a birth is very rare in this world.
verses.commentary: Even more fortunate than birth in a wealthy family is birth into a family of spiritually advanced yogis. Such a birth provides direct spiritual guidance from childhood and is considered extremely rare. This verse shows that advanced seekers are given the most conducive possible circumstances to complete their spiritual journey, confirming that divine grace preserves and supports every sincere spiritual effort.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
तत्र तं बुद्धिसंयोगं लभते पौर्वदेहिकम्।
यतते च ततो भूयः संसिद्धौ कुरुनन्दन॥
verses.transliteration:
Tatra Tam Buddhi-Samyogam Labhate Paurva-Dehikam.
Yatate Cha Tato Bhuyah Samsiddhau Kuru-Nandana.
verses.hindiTranslation: वहाँ उसे पूर्व जन्म की बुद्धि (संस्कार) का संयोग प्राप्त होता है, और हे कुरुनन्दन! फिर वह सिद्धि के लिए और अधिक प्रयत्न करता है।
verses.englishTranslation: There one regains the wisdom and spiritual impressions from the previous body. Then one strives again for perfection, O joy of the Kurus.
verses.commentary: In the new birth, the yoga-bhrashta naturally reconnects with the spiritual wisdom accumulated in previous lives. These dormant impressions (samskaras) resurface as an innate inclination toward spiritual life. From this foundation, the seeker continues striving toward perfection with even greater intensity. This verse explains why some individuals are naturally drawn to spirituality from a young age.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
पूर्वाभ्यासेन तेनैव ह्रियते ह्यवशोऽपि सः।
जिज्ञासुरपि योगस्य शब्दब्रह्मातिवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
Purva-Abhyasena Tena-Iva Hriyate Hy-Avasho'pi Sah.
Jijnasur-Api Yogasya Shabda-Brahma-Ativartate.
verses.hindiTranslation: पूर्व जन्म के अभ्यास से वह अवश (बिना प्रयास) होकर भी खिंचा चला जाता है। योग का जिज्ञासु भी शब्दब्रह्म (वैदिक कर्मकाण्ड) से ऊपर उठ जाता है।
verses.englishTranslation: By the force of previous practice, one is irresistibly drawn toward yoga. Even a mere inquirer about yoga transcends the ritualistic Vedic practices.
verses.commentary: Krishna reveals the remarkable power of spiritual practice from past lives — it creates an irresistible pull (hriyate) that draws the person back to yoga even without conscious effort. Furthermore, even someone who merely inquires about yoga surpasses the level of ritualistic religious practices. This shows the extraordinary value of yoga — even a little interest in it places one beyond conventional religiosity.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
प्रयत्नाद्यतमानस्तु योगी संशुद्धकिल्बिषः।
अनेकजन्मसंसिद्धस्ततो याति परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
Prayatnad-Yatamanah-Tu Yogi Samshuddha-Kilbishah.
Aneka-Janma-Samsiddhas-Tato Yati Param Gatim.
verses.hindiTranslation: परन्तु प्रयत्नपूर्वक अभ्यास करने वाला योगी, सम्पूर्ण पापों से शुद्ध होकर, अनेक जन्मों में सिद्ध होकर, परम गति (मोक्ष) को प्राप्त होता है।
verses.englishTranslation: But the yogi who strives with effort, purified of all sins, and perfected through many births, then attains the supreme destination.
verses.commentary: Krishna reveals that spiritual perfection may take many lifetimes of persistent effort, but it is ultimately assured. The yogi who continues to strive with sincerity, becoming progressively purified, eventually reaches the supreme destination. This teaching gives both patience and hope — perfection may not come in one lifetime, but the journey continues without loss across births until the final goal is reached.

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
तपस्विभ्योऽधिको योगी ज्ञानिभ्योऽपि मतोऽधिकः।
कर्मिभ्यश्चाधिको योगी तस्माद्योगी भवार्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
Tapasvibhyo'dhiko Yogi Jnanibhyo'pi Mato'dhikah.
Karmibhyash-Cha-Adhiko Yogi Tasmad-Yogi Bhava-Arjuna.
verses.hindiTranslation: योगी तपस्वियों से श्रेष्ठ है, ज्ञानियों से भी श्रेष्ठ माना गया है, और कर्मियों (कर्मकाण्डियों) से भी श्रेष्ठ है। इसलिए हे अर्जुन! तुम योगी बनो।
verses.englishTranslation: The yogi is greater than the ascetics, greater even than the scholars, and greater than the ritualists. Therefore, be a yogi, O Arjuna.
verses.commentary: Krishna establishes the supreme status of the yogi above three categories of spiritual practitioners: ascetics who practice penance, scholars who pursue knowledge, and ritualists who perform Vedic ceremonies. The yogi who combines inner communion with God, selfless action, and true knowledge surpasses all these. The chapter's penultimate instruction is direct and powerful: 'Tasmad yogi bhava Arjuna' — Therefore, become a yogi!

verses.speaker: Krishna
verses.devanagari:
योगिनामपि सर्वेषां मद्गतेनान्तरात्मना।
श्रद्धावान्भजते यो मां स मे युक्ततमो मतः॥
verses.transliteration:
Yoginam-Api Sarvesham Mad-Gatena-Antar-Atmana.
Shraddha-Van Bhajate Yo Mam Sa Me Yuktatamo Matah.
verses.hindiTranslation: सब योगियों में भी जो श्रद्धावान होकर अपने अन्तरात्मा को मुझमें लगाए हुए मुझे भजता है — वह मेरे मत में सबसे श्रेष्ठ योगी (युक्ततम) है।
verses.englishTranslation: And of all yogis, one who worships Me with faith, with the inner self absorbed in Me — that person is considered by Me to be the most united, the highest yogi.
verses.commentary: The chapter culminates with its most important declaration: among all yogis — ascetics, meditators, jnanis, and karma yogis — the highest is the devotee who worships Krishna with faith and whose inner being is absorbed in God. This verse elevates bhakti (devotion) to the supreme position, placing the personal relationship with God above all other spiritual practices. Faith (shraddha) and loving worship (bhajana) are declared the pinnacle of yoga.
